name: Brooklyn Style Pizza
method: In 1 cup of warm water, add yeast and let rest for 10 minutes. Bubbles will form on top. Mix flour and salt in a large bowl and add yeast water very slowly, mixing with your hands until you can handle the dough without sticking. Add more flour if necessary, and knead dough for 5 minutes. Cover with a towel and let rise for an hour or until doubled. Punch dough down and knead for 2-3 minutes, add flour if dough sticks. Let rise covered in warm place another hour.

In the meantime, while your dough is rising a second time, in a large frying pan on medium heat, add 2 T olive oil, half an onion, tomato paste and tomato passata. Add 1 can of water, salt & pepper, and oregano. Cover and let simmer on low for 20 minutes. Shred mozzarella and grate parmesan for the toppings. Set aside. Prepare a parchment-lined 12 inch pizza pan. Move oven rack to bottom and preheat oven to 460°degrees

After dough has risen 2nd time, punch down and turn onto a semolina-dusted cutting board or flat surface. Knead a few times, dough will be pretty stretchy. It is not stretchy when you make the wood oven pizza recipe because that style of pizza is Roman and it's a thin, hard, crunchy crust. This style is from Brooklyn and it's softer, so you can fold it. Stretch pizza onto your parchment-lined pizza pan and bake until bottom is evenly browned. It takes my oven about 10 minutes, time varies according to your oven, so just keep an eye on it, I stand by the oven with the light on and check on it a lot. The bottom is browned when you can lift it and see it evenly browned. Remove from oven.

Turn your oven to broil (500°F). Drizzle olive oil in a circular fashion from the center outward and then put a ladle or two of sauce in the middle and spread out in a circular fashion to cover the top. Please note that the sauce and cheese should be spread evenly. Sprinkle parmesan, then mozzarella. Broil for 5-8 minutes until brown and bubbly. Remove from oven and cut into slices with a pizza cutter. This was the most difficult pizza for me to make. If you look on the internet for pizza from New York the recipes are very particular, and some insist on using the refrigerator to let the dough rise for 24-48 hours. I tried a few times with no success, gave up for a little while , and then tried again adapting the dough a little. It achieved the desired effect and it tasted similar to Johnny & Vinny's on 18 Avenue. The water in New York is supposed to be different, so it will never taste exactly the same if your not there. Good luck everyone!
